Memory  
Fast Inter-Process Communication Using  
Shared Address Space

What is Shared Memory?  
• Fastest IPC mechanism  
• Multiple processes map the same memory region  
• Data is exchanged by direct read/write, not system calls  
• Created using shm_open() + mmap()  
• Shared region lives inside kernel-managed memory objects  
Process A  
Process B  
Shared Memory  
Why Use Shared Memory?  
Zero-copy → no kernel  
Ideal for large data  
transfers  
Highest throughput IPC  
copying overhead  
Great for:  
• sensor buffers  
• image frames  
• circular queues  
• control blocks  
• shared lookup tables  
POSIX Shared Memory Basics  
• Identified by a name beginning with /  
Example: /apex_shm  
• Behaves like a file descriptor  
• Must specify size using ftruncate()  
• Mapped into virtual memory using mmap()  
• Must be cleaned using shm_unlink()  
Key APIs  
• Create/Open:  
• int fd = shm_open("/apex", O_CREAT | O_RDWR, 0666);  
• Set Size:  
• ftruncate(fd, SIZE);  
• Map Memory:  
• void *ptr = mmap(NULL, SIZE, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE, MAP_SHARED, fd, 0);  
• Cleanup:  
• munmap(ptr, SIZE);  
• close(fd);  
• shm_unlink("/apex");.  
Lifecycle of Shared Memory Object  
shm_open()  
ftruncate()  
mmap()  
read/write  
munmap()  
shm_unlink()  
Mapping Shared Memory  
• mmap() parameters:  
• PROT_READ, PROT_WRITE  
• MAP_SHARED (changes visible to all processes)  
• Important:  
• Use MAP_SHARED (not MAP_PRIVATE) for IPC  
• Mapping returns pointer → use it like a normal C array/struct  
Example: Writer and Reader Process  
int fd = shm_open("/apex", O_CREAT | O_RDWR, 0666);  
ftruncate(fd, 1024);  
char *p = mmap(NULL, 1024, PROT_READ|PROT_WRITE,  
MAP_SHARED, fd, 0);  
sprintf(p, "Hello from writer!");  
int fd = shm_open("/apex", O_RDONLY, 0666);  
char *p = mmap(NULL, 1024, PROT_READ, MAP_SHARED,  
fd, 0);  
printf("Reader got: %s\n", p);  
Synchronization Required  
Shared memory gives fast sharing but introduces race conditions.  
• Examples requiring mutex/sem:  
• Updating shared counters  
• Writing multi-field structures  
• Producer-consumer buffers  
• Logging queues  
Shared memory ≠ thread-safe.  
Using Shared Memory with Mutex  
Steps:  
1.Put mutex structure inside shared memory  
PTHREAD_PROCESS_SHARED  
2.Initialize with  
attribute  
3.Multiple processes can lock/unlock it  
Diagram:  
[Shared Memory] → struct { mutex; data; }  
Unmapping & Cleaning Up  
• munmap(ptr, size) → detach mapping  
• close(fd) → release descriptor  
• shm_unlink(name) → delete shared-memory object  
• Important:  
• unlink should be done by creator or cleanup tool.

data

time

Example 2: Shared Integer Counter  
Task:  
•
Writer initializes a counter to 0  
Reader process increments every second  
Writer prints current value every second  
Observe shared updates  
•
•
•
Learning: shared variables without copying  
Example 3: Producer–Consumer Buffer  
Shared memory contains:  
•
circular buffer (array)  
two indexes: head, tail  
two semaphores (empty, full)  
•
•
Implement: Producer writes items; consumer prints them.  
Learning: synchronization + shared data structure  
Real-World Usa Cases  
• Shared vision/image buffer between processes  
• Sensor data accumulation shared with control loop  
• Shared configuration block for real-time apps  
• Logging service shared across modules  
• Robotics (shared state, map, telemetry)  
Common Pitfalls  
• Forgetting ftruncate() → 0-size mapping  
• Using MAP_PRIVATE → changes not shared  
• Not synchronizing writes → corrupted structures  
• Forgetting shm_unlink() → orphaned objects remain  
• Writing beyond allocated size → segmentation faults  
• Multiple writers without locks → race conditions  
Troubleshooting  
• Use ls -l /dev/shm to list shared memory objects  
• Use ipcs -m (System V, optional for comparison)  
• Check return values of all system calls  
• Use strace for debugging open/mmap calls  
• Use hexdump to inspect raw memory if needed  
Summary  
• Fastest inter-process communication method  
• shm_open + mmap create shared memory region  
• Multiple processes map same address space → read/write directly  
• Requires explicit synchronization  
• Ideal for high-performance embedded systems  
Scenario: Telemetry Ring Buffer with Dual  
Semaphores  
(1/4)  
Context  
• Implement a high-rate telemetry pipeline where a producer process samples data (e.g., random sensor  
values) and writes into a shared memory circular buffer; a consumer process reads, computes rolling  
statistics (min/max/avg), and prints them.  
• Synchronization via counting semaphores empty and full (+ a mutex for head/tail if you choose).  
Scenario: Telemetry Ring Buffer with Dual  
Semaphores  
(2/4)  
PART 1 — Shared Region & Metadata  
• Create telemetry_common.h, producer.c, consumer.c.  
• Shared memory object /apex_shm_telemetry, size enough for:  
• struct header { int head, tail; int capacity; }  
• struct sample { uint64_t ts_ns; float value; } buf[CAP];  
• Initialize with ftruncate and mmap in producer (creator).  
Scenario: Telemetry Ring Buffer with Dual  
Semaphores  
(3/4)  
PART 2 — Synchronization  
• Semaphores in shared memory or named semaphores:  
• empty = CAP, full = 0.  
• Producer flow (loop @ 100 Hz):  
• sem_wait(empty) → write sample at head → advance head (mod CAP) → sem_post(full)  
• Consumer flow (loop):  
• sem_wait(full) → read sample at tail → advance tail → sem_post(empty)  
• Update sliding window stats and print every second.  
Scenario: Telemetry Ring Buffer with Dual  
Semaphores  
(4/4)  
PART 3 — Graceful Shutdown  
• Handle SIGINT: producer writes a shutdown flag in header; consumer exits when queue drained.  
• Cleanup: munmap, close, shm_unlink (producer), sem_close/sem_unlink if named.
